title: DHS Mandates Body Cameras on All ICE Arrest Teams Following Fatal Shootings
excerpt: DHS announced that at least one officer on every ICE arrest team must wear an activated body camera before a vehicle stop can proceed, following two consecutive fatal shootings where agents had no cameras. Full deployment to all field offices was promised within 60 days.
content:
On July 15, 2026, the Department of Homeland Security announced a new operational mandate requiring every ICE arrest team to include at least one officer wearing an active body camera before conducting a vehicle stop. The announcement came directly after the fatal shootings of Salgado Araujo in Houston and Durán Guerrero in Maine, in which none of the officers involved were wearing cameras.

The mandate exposed a significant accountability gap: despite Congress appropriating $20 million for body cameras in April 2026, and DHS having issued an internal directive requiring camera use as far back as February 2025 (Directive 19010.3), as of March 2026 only about 3,000 of 13,000 ICE agents — fewer than one in four — were actually equipped. DHS blamed funding lapses caused by government shutdowns for the incomplete rollout.

  • Border Czar Tom Homan confirmed on Fox News the cameras had been "purchased" and that instructors were being trained for nationwide deployment.
  • DHS said remaining field offices would receive cameras within 60 days; Acting ICE Director David Venturella told Rep. Sylvia Garcia that all agents in the field would have cameras by the end of July.
  • The mandate applies specifically to vehicle stops; agents conducting home visits or workplace raids are not yet covered by the specific vehicle-stop requirement.
  • Critics noted DHS had made nearly identical promises after the January 2026 Minneapolis shootings of U.S. citizens Renee Good and Alex Pretti — and failed to fulfill them.
